audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Pilate, Barabbas, crowd dynamics, mocking, crucifixion, Psalm lament, death, veil tearing, centurion confession, women witnesses, and Joseph burial.

verses.restored: Immediately in the morning, the chief priests with the elders, scribes, and whole council took counsel. Binding Jesus, they led him away and handed him over to Pilate.
Pilate questioned him, Are you the king of the Judeans? He answered him, You say it.
The chief priests were accusing him of many things.
Pilate again questioned him, saying, Do you answer nothing? See how many things they accuse you of.
But Jesus no longer answered anything, so that Pilate marveled.
At the feast he would release for them one prisoner whom they requested.
There was one called Barabbas, bound with the rebels who had committed murder in the uprising.
The crowd came up and began to ask him to do as he usually did for them.
Pilate answered them, saying, Do you want me to release for you the king of the Judeans?
For he knew that the chief priests had handed him over because of envy.
But the chief priests stirred up the crowd so that he would release Barabbas for them instead.
Pilate again answered and said to them, What then do you want me to do with the one you call king of the Judeans?
They cried out again, Crucify him.
Pilate said to them, Why, what harm has he done? But they cried out even more, Crucify him.
Pilate, wishing to satisfy the crowd, released Barabbas for them; and after flogging Jesus, he handed him over to be crucified.
The soldiers led him away inside the courtyard, that is, the praetorium, and called together the whole cohort.
They clothed him in purple, and twisting a thorn crown, they placed it on him.
They began to greet him, Hail, king of the Judeans.
They struck his head with a reed, spat on him, and kneeling, bowed before him.
When they had mocked him, they stripped off the purple and put his own garments on him, and they led him out to crucify him.
They compel one passing by, Simon of Cyrene, coming from the country, the father of Alexander and Rufus, to carry his cross.
They bring him to the place Golgotha, which is translated, Place of a Skull.
They offered him wine mixed with myrrh, but he did not take it.
They crucify him and divide his garments, casting lots for them, who should take what.
It was the third hour, and they crucified him.
The inscription of the charge against him was written: The King of the Judeans.
With him they crucify two robbers, one at his right and one at his left.
[Some later manuscripts add: And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, He was counted with lawless ones.]
Those passing by blasphemed him, shaking their heads and saying, Ha! You who destroy the temple and build it in three days,
save yourself by coming down from the cross.
Likewise the chief priests with the scribes mocked among themselves, saying, He saved others; himself he cannot save.
Let the Messiah, the king of Israel, come down now from the cross, so that we may see and trust. Those crucified with him also reproached him.
When the sixth hour came, darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour.
At the ninth hour Jesus cried out with a great voice, Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani? which is translated, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
Some of those standing by heard and said, Look, he calls Elijah.
Someone ran, filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a reed, and gave him drink, saying, Leave him; let us see whether Elijah comes to take him down.
Jesus, releasing a great voice, breathed out.
The veil of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom.
When the centurion standing opposite him saw that in this way he breathed out, he said, Truly this human was Son of the Divine.
There were also women watching from afar, among whom were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the Little and Joses, and Salome.
When he was in Galilee, they followed him and served him, and many other women came up with him to Jerusalem.
When evening had already come, since it was Preparation, that is, the day before Sabbath,
Joseph of Arimathea, a respected council member who himself was waiting for the reign of the Divine, came boldly to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.
Pilate marveled that he had already died; calling the centurion, he asked whether he had been dead for some time.
Learning from the centurion, he granted the corpse to Joseph.
Buying a linen cloth, he took him down, wrapped him in the linen cloth, and placed him in a tomb that had been cut from rock. He rolled a stone against the door of the tomb.
Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses were watching where he was placed.
